speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, this is tape four. Albert, I'd just like to go back first to the camp. Where did you learn to speak Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I learn?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There in the camp. You know, in the evening, we go together, you know, and some, uh, volunteer teacher from the camp, you know, they teach us Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any special friends in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only these immigrants, nothing, uh, different, not a Russian. The Russian people, the local people, they distance from us, you know? You know, they was very afraid to make friendship with foreigners, you know? They're Russian. They don't-- was very afraid to come in contact with foreigners, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the Russian-- the people in authority? Were they very difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the authority? They were not actually difficult. It's why we have the documents. We are-- permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't-- were they cruel? I mean, did they beat you or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no. It was-- different was when we work, you know, as help worker, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the farm, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- talk with us-- no, not when there was the other one, only when he was alone. No weakness, you know? There were always this, you know, from the interior police, you know? There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I must tell, the Russian people, the normal Russians, they was very nice. No, not nice, and the big antisemites was the Ukraine, you know? They was this. So when I come to central Asia, you know, to Tajikistan, when I worked there, I find I was again lucky. I have the money. It is-- I walk-- were small-- not a room, a bed, you know, in the kitchen, you know? And I pay for this a few rubles, you know? So I walk to the work, you know, for the brick factory and back. So-- Can you-- yes, carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I said in 1942, I got the permission, go to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, tell me, was there any-- when you say they were antisemitic, did they do anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no. I don't show him the documents. I-- I tell I am from Czechoslovakia, you know. The documents, I show only the interior police, not the private people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them do anything to any other Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- Jewish [INAUDIBLE], started in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Tajikistan. There was a lot Polish people, Polish Jewish people. And there was a lot people that throw away from the Baltic states, you know? When the Soviets occupied the Baltic states, Litwa, Latvia, you know, they threw away first the Jewish all in Asia, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So starting about, yes, Tajikistan and Kurdistan and all these Asian republic, they were full with people from, um, West-- East Ukraine and from the Baltics. It is what happens now. You know, there are in-- in the Baltic states, you know, when it come to voting something, there are more Russian than local people, you know? While the Russian put the Russian people in the Latvia-- in the Baltic states, you know? And the original people that-- throw out, you know? So it is Latvia. No, nearly 60% or 70% from the inhabits are not Latvian. They are Russians, you know? It's-- it's very complicated with the-- the politics. So come back to Buzuluk in the army. No, you were telling me about-- did they-- were they antisemitic to the people from-- the Polish Jews? Did they do anything to the Polish Jews in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not so. They-- they came to work hard, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't hit them or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember to. No, in this-- I must tell, in this, I speak about the Russia. I never-- nobody hit me, all the time, no different. You know, there was a special-- when they started to you-- you know, the [INAUDIBLE], interior police. You must stay on the corridor with the face to the wall and their hands on the wall, you know? It was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you let-- they let you go stay one, two hours, you know? And after the [INAUDIBLE], started to ask you, you know? Always started here, then jumped back and come again, you know, to confuse you. So-- but it wasn't difficult to confuse me, [INAUDIBLE]. So go back to the army. Unless you have anything else to tell me at the camp. Anything else you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I say in Tajikistan, it was within Jewish prisoner, you know, what about the religion. So there was a lot. No, you can't-- you can't see, is-- he's a Tajik or he's a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing was the different was the Friday and-- and Saturday, you know? You can see there was-- were a little bit different, the Jewish on Saturday, and different on Friday the origin people, you know? When you say they were different, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they changed their clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they don't work. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't seen in Tajikistan a synagogue. I never have seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were allowed not to work on-- on the Saturday? The Russians didn't mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't-- I was always in the brick factory, [INAUDIBLE], not in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the Polish Jews stay again and the Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't mix all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no mix. The Polish people, when there was the Polish army, you know, they go to the Polish army, you know? No, there was mixed. They was from Poland, you know, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so tell me about the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I start at the army. I got back my-- these. In 1943, started-- we go to the front, yes? And after, come back to Novo [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go to the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the front?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us with the train, you know, and walking, again. So we started in-- near Kharkov, you know? In the army, in the Czech army, the first beginning, we have been over thousand people. From these thousand people-- I will remember the place. We're near to Kharkov-- was nearly 700 Jewish, you know? All Jewish, Czechoslovak Jewish. And their died, I don't know, few hundreds, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: We come back. And it was the per-- permission from the Soviets that organize the brigade, you know? So they took me to the tankist. So I was a tankist. So in-- I don't remember the year. What time? Spring?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43-- it was spring. We go to the front, you know, and we helped liberate Kiev, the town Kiev on the Ukraine, yes, the Czechoslovak tankist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's difficult to tell, you know? You stay in the tanks and move and move and move. You don't see nothing. You use this. No, it's very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we finished, stopped behind, uh, Kiev in a small-- small town, so a big village. And I was ill, you know? And they took me to the Soviet-- not hospital, lazarette or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now what happens, all the Soviets, they take the people, the ill people, inside to Russia. So I was afraid when they took me to Russia, I never come back. So in the night, you know, I run away from this lazarette back to the Czechs. We'd [INAUDIBLE] with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was their-- our doctor that give me something. So in two, three days, I was again all right. Now in between, they further me. I was from the sergeant, eh, lieutenant, you know? From there, we go to Poland, you know? And when we come to Poland I was always looking in the camps, you know, for my family-- nobody. So we come from Poland. The tankists, the army go to the south, you know? Tell me about the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about, you were looking for your family, you say, in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when we go with the army, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there was small camps, you know? German these, yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Full with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody there, I tell you, I will.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go to the-- from Poland down to Bukovina and Czernowitz. We stayed there, and from there go to the [? Carpaths, ?] you know, and cross the [? Carpathia, ?] yes, with all small this. Yes, to the [? Carpaths, ?] to Czechoslovakia, Slovakia. And we stayed a tankist near Kosice, you know? How you call this? Kezmarok, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the war was going. The Soviets took the tankist to Poland. They order army, you know, they stay in Czechoslovakia, go to Czechoslovakia. Only the tankists go back to Poland, to Nowy Targ, and go with the Soviets under Soviet commander, you know? And one time we stopped, you know. It was near to Auschwitz. So what we have done-- we run straight to Auschwitz, you know? It was few hours after it was liberated, you know, Auschwitz. What we have seen, you know, the films and the Holocaust, you can't tell you, you know? Half an hour after they were liberated, you know? So again, I look some relatives. And nobody was there. Can you tell me about Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: About Auschwitz? I was a few-- few hours only. I can tell you one thing. My late wife, she was same, in the army, you know? When we came there, she took my hand and told me, Albert, you know, I am from an Orthodox Jewish family, and my parents. Now when I see these chimneys and see just what I can see, I hope my parents forgive me. I don't believe in God. Say [INAUDIBLE], I don't say the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: We grow up two daughters. I never-- they know we are Jewish. I don't change my name. I don't change their religion, though I wasn't a member of synagogue. No, always Jewish, you know? So we grew up the children. They know they-- we are Jewish, though not member of synagogue. What happens? It is-- I make a jump, you know? It is 10 or 15 years ago. I come in London to my daughter, you know, and ask, 'Anna, where are the children?' She started laughing. Said '[INAUDIBLE], you are laughing. You don't believe it. The children are in the Sunday school in the synagogue.' So in the third generation goes my grandchildren back to the religion. So I asked my daughter, 'Anna, what are you saying?' So I did nothing. It is not my business. It's the business for my children. They like it. They like going to the synagogue. I am very happy. So what happens in the third generation? My children go back to the Jewish religion. So let me take you back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I found nobody was there. My late wife, you know-- I was officer-- goes to the commander, you know, and asks him for a lorry. So what she have done, she took some people from the town where she was born and from Jelenia, and took on the lorry and took them back to Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: After this, I was travel while the front was moving. And the lorry wasn't there. So I go to my commander and told him-- told him the truth, that my wife took the lorry to take some people from the-- Auschwitz to Slovakia. So it was all-- so we go further, further, further with the front, from Poland to Germany, to Ratibor, and from Germany back to Silesia to Troppau, yes, and from Troppau to Os-- Ostrow-- yes, Ostrow, you know? This was a Czech town. And there, I finished the war, you know? They asked me, the army-- in between, I go further and further, you know? So I finished the war. Actually, then a captain, you know? So they ask to sign that I will stay in the army as professional. And I don't sign it. I don't want this. So they take me to the tank brigade in Slovakia, in Martin, near to Zilina, you know? There was nothing there. So I asked to let me out from the army. So I finished the army actually sometime in June or July 1945. So after, in a few weeks, you know, I got a letter. It was by mistake. They let me out from the army. [INAUDIBLE] sorry, I have the letter. I am free from the army. Army-- only when I knew war will be-- I come back to the army. I am over the age, so I don't need. So they let me out. OK, let's go back a bit. What was life like in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I like?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what was it like, for you? Were the conditions terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we have enough to eat, you know? The uniform and the transport, it was from Britain. So we have the battle dress-- British battle dress in the army, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And most of these meals, what we eat, it come from Britain-- Britain via Persia, in the east [INAUDIBLE] you know? So for eating, you know, was no trouble, you know? Sometimes was trouble on the front, you know, normal in this, you know? My trouble-- this wasn't a trouble. I was-- I  don't know-- adjutant to my commander, yes? My brigade commander had three adjutants. I was one of the-- from the three. So what happens? There comes prisoners, you know, [INAUDIBLE]. So I was only who speak German, you know? So when I must ask the first question to the German. So I must-- some dirty tricks, you know? When I ask him, where you are? So he said, I am a soldier from the Wehrmacht. [INAUDIBLE]. There were some SS or SR, you know? So I have seen. You know, they have the signs under their arm. So I have a soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, these prisoners, you know, some soldiers, they took it, give it to the Russian. Know to-- to took it to the Russian, you need two, three days to take them and bring him back, then come back. No, when I have seen it was an SS, you know, or an SR men, there's mostly lie. So I take this soldier and tell him, take these men to the Russian, give it up. No, you must be back in a half hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well he knows what happens. He was back in the half hour. So I tell him, why you are so early, [INAUDIBLE], now you know. He went to run away so I shoot him. So this was my dirty trick, you know? Only to the SS. And what was the name of your commander?

speaker: interviewee
text: Janko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Janko was my commander.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Czech. And he took his life in 1968 when the Russia liberated us the second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to go back, so I was out of the army. No, wait, wait a minute. I want to go back a little bit. Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I [INAUDIBLE] my wife, I-- I leave my wife in the, uh, farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Met her. No, she was married, you know? So I started a friendship. No, her husband die-- died in the war, in the first front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't remember when I married. It was difficult to marry without documents. No, we started-- stay together. She was with me in the army, yes, and come back to the army. And I married-- I don't know. Well, around there, '44, '45, you know? And she was with you in-- she was-- was she with you in the tanks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. She stayed in the back. She come after-- after Kiev, when we have been, you know? She was still in Buzuluk, you know, or the-- the part of the army was there. So she stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you liberated Kiev, were the Russians-- did they make great celebrations or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at Kiev, they make a big celebration. They give the-- there was three-- I don't know how you say in English-- three groups, it was the big tanks, yes, the small tanks, and the other group, you know? So all these commanders from the three groups, they got to the big Russian orders, you know? And the commander got same, a Lenin order. And I got the first War Cross, [POINTS], this one, yes, the Czechoslovak War-- and again, I go one higher with this. So I finished the war, what I say, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a minute. Tell me-- is there anything else you can tell me about being in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else about the army? Were they mainly Jewish people in your brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the brigade was Jewish people. And what I forgot in between, you know, to Czechoslovakia belonged the so-called Carpatho-Rusland. This was a part of Czechoslovakia. And in this war time before, when the Russian did, they took away this Carpatho-Rus, you know, and took it in the forced labor camps. And in '42-- no, [INAUDIBLE]-- yes, '42, they liberated them and they come in the Czechoslovak army. So there was a lot of, uh, Czechoslovak Ukraine, yes? Zakarpatska Ukraine they call it. So there was Czechs, Slovaks, and this. And after-- I don't remember a bit. In Slovakia have the Slovak army. And there was, uh, fight against the Russian. No, the same-- they run away and come to us, to the Czechoslovak army. So there was a lot of Slovaks too.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czechoslovak army was very mixed. You know, there was a lot of Czechs, origin Czechs, a lot of origin Slovaks, this Carpatho-Rus people and Czechoslovak Jewish people, you know, like I was. Yes, I was a Czechoslovak citizen-- no not Jewish nationality, you know? And you commanded them in Czechoslovakian. You spoke Czech to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you spoke to them, you spoke in Czech?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czech, everything in Czech. Not in Slovak, all in Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll just stop the tape there, OK? Thank you.
